1: YHWH speaks to both Moses and Aaron — the only chapter in Leviticus explicitly addressed to both. The pairing is significant: the diagnostic role belongs to the priest (Aaron's line), while Moses transmits the legal framework. The dual address establishes that priestly authority over ritual purity is covenantal, not merely functional.
2: sebeth o sappachat o baheret (śeʾet ō-sappaḥat ō-baheret): three skin-condition terms whose precise distinctions remain debated. Sebeth (from nasa = to lift/carry) denotes a swelling or elevation in the skin. Sappachat (from saphach = to attach/join) is a secondary eruption or scab — something that clings to or spreads from the primary mark. Baheret (from bahar = to be bright/white) is a bright white spot. Together they form the range of surface presentations that may indicate nega tzaraat (negaʿ ṣāraʿat) — literally a blow or plague of tzaraat. Nega (from naga = to touch, strike, afflict) covers any afflicted area; its use implies something divinely inflicted. Tzaraat is traditionally rendered leprosy but is broader — it covers various skin conditions including vitiligo, psoriasis, and fungal infections; modern Hansen's disease was likely unknown in ancient Israel. The priest is the diagnostic authority: not a physician but a ritual judge who determines whether the condition crosses the threshold of covenant-disqualifying impurity.
3: veraah hakkohen et-hanega beohr habasar: the priest shall see/examine (raah) the mark. The verb raah (to see, examine, consider) carries the full weight of judicial examination — the same word used for prophetic vision and divine observation. The priest is not diagnosing disease medically but rendering a ritual verdict. The key diagnostic signs are: (1) sear halabban (śeʿar lābān) — white hair (hair in the mark has turned white), and (2) mareh hanega amok mivor habasar — the appearance of the mark is deeper than the surrounding skin. Both signs indicate active, penetrating affliction. The verdict formula nega tzaraat hu — it is a mark of tzaraat — is the priestly pronouncement that places the person in the unclean/quarantine category.
4: vehibdilo hakkohen shivat yamim: the priest shall isolate (hibdil) the person for seven days. Hibdil is the same root as havdalah (the Sabbath separation ceremony) — to separate, set apart. The diagnostic isolation of seven days mirrors the creation week: a full cycle of time in which the condition either resolves or develops. If the baheret (white spot) has not spread and the hair has not turned white, the case remains uncertain — it is a sappachat (scall), not full tzaraat, and requires further observation. The seven-day observation period builds discernment time into the diagnostic process, preventing premature condemnation or premature clearance.
5: vehibdilo shivat yamim sheniyot: a second seven-day isolation period is required if the mark has neither spread nor changed. The doubling of the observation period (7 + 7 = 14 days) reflects the gravity of the verdict: the priest must be sure before pronouncing someone tameh. This judicial caution — requiring multiple observations before a life-altering verdict — models the legal principle of careful witness and examination.
6: vetiher otho hakkohen: the priest shall pronounce him clean (tiher, from tahor = pure, clean). If the mark has faded and not spread, the condition is diagnosed as sappachat — a secondary eruption, not full tzaraat — and the priest declares the person clean. He must wash (kibbes) his garments and is then ritually clean. The washing of garments is a standard purity-restoration action, signaling that the contamination was real but bounded. The priest's declaration tahor hu (he is clean) is not medical opinion but priestly verdict — it restores full participation in the covenant community.
7: ve-im pasoah tifsheh hasappachat baohr: if the scall has at all spread (pasoh tifsheh — an infinitive absolute construction indicating emphasis and certainty of the spreading). The spreading is the diagnostic trigger for upgrading the verdict: what was a sappachat (minor eruption) now displays the hallmark of true tzaraat. The priest re-examines and re-pronounces. The double appearance before the priest — first at the initial examination, second if the condition changes — ensures that both clearance and condemnation require priestly oversight.
8: vetimaahu hakkohen tzaraat hu: the priest shall declare him unclean — tzaraat hu (it is tzaraat). This is the formal diagnosis that places a person in the category of those requiring isolation, special garments, and verbal self-announcement. Tameh (ṭāmēʾ) in the priestly system is not primarily a moral category but a ritual one — it describes a state of incompatibility with the holy. Those who are tameh cannot participate in worship, approach the tabernacle, or eat sacred food. The declaration is the priest's verbal act of categorization — performative speech that changes the person's social and religious status.
9: nega tzaraat ki tihyeh beadam: when a mark of tzaraat is in a person (beadam = in/on a human being, from adam, emphasizing the person's full humanity). This opening verse of the second diagnostic section introduces tzaraat noshenet — chronic, longstanding skin disease. The distinction between new/acute conditions (Leviticus 13:2-8) and chronic/established conditions (Leviticus 13:9-17) reflects a diagnostic wisdom: established disease that has reached a stable state may be treated differently from active, spreading disease.
10: sebeth lebana (white swelling) combined with sear laban (white hair in the swelling) and michyat basar chai basebeth — raw flesh in the swelling. Michyat (from michyah) refers to quick/raw flesh — living, exposed tissue. The combination of white swelling + white hair + raw flesh is diagnostic for full tzaraat. Basar chai (living/raw flesh) appearing within the mark is the key additional sign: the disease has penetrated to the living layer beneath the skin surface, indicating active, deep affliction.
11: tzaraat noshenet hi beohr besaro vetimaenu hakkohen lo yisgirenu ki tameh hu: chronic tzaraat — it is an old/established tzaraat in the skin of his body. The priest shall declare him unclean without isolating him, because he is already/clearly unclean. The diagnostic certainty of established disease removes the need for the 7-day observation period. Noshenet (from yashon = to be old/established) — the condition has been present long enough that its nature is not in doubt. This is the classic case of tzaraat that required no further observation: the verdict was self-evident.
12: ve-im paroach tifroach hatzaraat baohr: if the tzaraat has completely broken out over the skin — paroach tifroach (infinitive absolute for emphasis). When the disease has covered the entire body surface from head to foot in white, a paradoxical rule applies: the person may be declared clean. This seemingly counterintuitive ruling reflects the diagnostic logic: if the entire surface has turned white (kuloh hifoch laban), the raw flesh that indicates active disease is no longer visible. The condition has reached a kind of completion — it is not spreading, not showing raw flesh. This edge case demonstrates the sophisticated medical-legal reasoning of the priestly diagnostic system.
13: tahor hu: he is clean — the paradox of the fully blanched patient declared clean. The priest examines and seeing the entire surface white, pronounces clean. The principle appears to be: active infection shows red/raw flesh; the all-white condition is a static, resolved state even if visually dramatic. This ruling has generated significant rabbinic discussion and suggests the tzaraat regulations are not naive descriptions of disease progression but carefully worked-out ritual categories.
14: uveyom hera'ot bo basar chai yitma: on the day that raw flesh (basar chai) appears in it, he shall be unclean. The appearance of raw flesh reverses the clean verdict immediately. The raw flesh is the key diagnostic sign of active disease — open, living, exposed tissue indicates ongoing affliction. The system is dynamic: a person's status can change when the observable signs change. This mutability requires ongoing priestly oversight, not a one-time determination.
15: veraah hakkohen et habasar hachai vetimaenu basar hachai tameh hu tzaraat hu: the priest examines the raw flesh and declares unclean — raw flesh is unclean, it is tzaraat. The repetition of the diagnosis formula underscores the diagnostic principle: basar chai (raw/living flesh) = tzaraat = tameh. The formula serves as a diagnostic rule that priests could apply in any case where raw flesh appeared.
16: o chi yashov habasar hachai venehpach lelaban: or if the raw flesh turns back and changes to white. The reversibility continues: if the raw flesh later becomes white (the sign that active disease has resolved), the person shall come again to the priest.
17: vetiharah hakkohen tahor hu: the priest declares him clean — he is clean. The dynamic back-and-forth between tameh and tahor status depending on the observable condition of the skin marks represents a responsive system: status follows reality. The priest's ongoing access for re-examination means that isolation is not permanent by default; it is reassessed as the condition changes.
18: vebasar ki yihyeh vo shechin: and when there is a boil (shechin) in the skin. A new category: disease arising in the site of a previous boil. Shechin (from a root meaning to be hot/burn) is an inflammatory skin infection. The boil itself is not tzaraat, but the site of a healed boil can become a locus of tzaraat-like symptoms. This specificity — categorizing conditions by their etiology and location — demonstrates the detailed observational basis of the priestly diagnostic system.
19: sebeth lebana o baheret lebana adamdemet: a white swelling or white-reddish bright spot. A new color term appears: adamdemet (reddish-white) — from adom = red. The combination of white and reddish suggests an inflamed border around a white center, or a bicolored lesion. This variant presentation at the site of a previous boil requires the same priestly examination as primary tzaraat.
20: im-mareh shafal min-haohr vesear hefach laban vetimaenu hakkohen nega tzaraat hi bashechin parach: if the appearance is lower than the skin and the hair has turned white, the priest shall declare him unclean — it is a mark of tzaraat that has broken out in the boil. The same diagnostic signs as primary tzaraat (depth below skin level + white hair) applied to the boil-site confirm tzaraat diagnosis.
21: ve-im yireenu hakkohen vehineh ein-bo sear laban ushefalah einenu min-haohr vehiv kehavah: but if the priest examines it and there is no white hair in it and it is not lower than the skin but has become dim/faded. Kehavah (dim, faded) indicates a resolving lesion without the active diagnostic signs. This condition is isolated for seven days — the same precautionary observation period as uncertain initial presentations.
22: ve-im pasoah tifsheh baohr: if it spreads in the skin, the priest shall declare him unclean. The spreading criterion applies across all skin conditions — spreading = active disease = tzaraat = tameh.
23: ve-im-tachteha ta'amod habaheret lo farach tzaraat hi uvahe zakah: but if the bright spot stays in its place and has not spread, it is the scar (tzavevet) of the boil — the priest shall declare him clean. The non-spreading bright spot at the boil site is diagnosed as cicatrix (scar tissue from healing), not tzaraat. This distinction between scar tissue and active disease is clinically perceptive: healed scars often produce white tissue that resembles the baheret but lacks the diagnostic signs of active tzaraat.
24: o basar ki yihyeh vo michvat-esh: or when there is a burn (michvat-esh = burning of fire) in the skin. Michvat (from kavah = to burn) and esh (fire) together describe a burn wound. Like the boil site, the burn site can develop tzaraat-like presentations. The range of etiologies — natural skin conditions, post-boil, post-burn — shows the comprehensiveness of the diagnostic framework.
25: veraah oto hakkohen vehineh nehpach sear laban babaheret umaraeha amok min-haohr tzaraat hi bamichvah parach: same diagnostic formula as before — white hair in the bright spot + appearance deeper than skin = tzaraat in the burn wound. The consistent application of the same diagnostic criteria across different etiological backgrounds demonstrates the underlying principle: the nature of the condition (its observable markers) determines the verdict, not its origin.
26: ve-im lo yireeh bakohenet sear laban: but if no white hair is seen in the bright spot and it is not lower than the skin. The absence of the two primary diagnostic signs leads to the precautionary 7-day isolation. The system defaults to protective isolation when evidence is ambiguous — a harm-reduction principle built into the diagnostic protocol.
27: The spreading criterion again determines the verdict: if the baheret has spread in seven days, the priest declares tameh — tzaraat. The uniformity of the spreading test across all presentations demonstrates its central diagnostic significance.
28: ve-im tachteha ta'amod habaheret lo farach baohr vehiv kehavah sebeth hamichvah hi: if the bright spot stays in its place and has not spread in the skin but is dim, it is the swelling of the burn — the priest declares him clean. Sebeth hamichvah (swelling/scarring of the burn) — the same principle as boil-site: non-spreading, fading lesions at a burn site are diagnosed as healing scar tissue, not tzaraat.
29: ve-ish o isha ki yihyeh vo nega berosh o vezaqan: a man or a woman when there is a mark in the head or in the beard. The head and facial hair regions present a specific diagnostic challenge: nithak (neteq) is the term for a scaly/ringworm-like condition of the scalp or beard area, distinct from the general tzaraat of the body surface. The specificity of nithak as a diagnostic subcategory reflects observation that scalp and beard conditions have distinctive presentations.
30: veraah hakkohen et-hanega vehineh maraehu amok min-haohr uso dak tzahov tzaraat harosh o-hazzaqan hu: if the appearance is deeper than the skin and there is fine/thin yellow-gold hair in it — it is a scall (nithak) of the head or beard. The distinctive diagnostic sign here is seara dak tzahov (fine, thin, yellow/golden hair) rather than white hair — a color variant specific to this head/beard condition. The fine yellow hair suggests the hair follicle itself has been affected differently than in body-surface tzaraat.
31: vehibdilo et-nega hanithak shivat yamim: the priest shall isolate the person with the nithak-mark for seven days. If the nithak has not spread and there is no yellow hair in it, the priest keeps the affected area shaved except the nithak-mark itself, and the person is isolated a second seven days for reassessment.
32: veraah hakkohen et-hanega bayom hashevii vehineh lo-farash hanithak: on the seventh day the priest examines — if the nithak has not spread and there is no yellow hair and its appearance is not deeper than the skin. The clean/unclear diagnostic criteria applied to nithak parallel those for body-surface tzaraat, but adapted for the scalp/beard context.
33: vehitgalach veet-hanithak lo yegalach vehibdilo hakkohen et-hanithak shivat yamim sheniyot: he shall shave himself, but he shall not shave the nithak itself, and the priest shall isolate the nithak an additional seven days. The prohibition against shaving the affected area preserves the diagnostic evidence: if the yellow-fine hair fell out, the diagnostic criterion would be lost. The second isolation period serves the same function as in the body-surface cases.
34: veraah hakkohen et-hanithak bayom hashevii vehineh lo-farash hanithak baohr umaraehu einenu amok min-haohr vetiher oto hakkohen: on the seventh day — if the nithak has not spread and its appearance is not deeper than the skin, the priest declares clean. The clean verdict requires non-spreading + non-deepening — the same two-sign criterion throughout the chapter.
35: ve-im pasoah yifsheh hanithak baohr achar taharo: but if the nithak should spread in the skin after his cleansing. The possibility of relapse after a clean verdict — and the requirement to return to the priest if this occurs — demonstrates that clean status is not permanent if the condition recurs. The ongoing relationship between patient and priest is a dynamic monitoring system, not a one-time determination.
36: veraah oto hakkohen vehineh parash hanithak baohr lo-yevaqer hakkohen lasear hatzahov tameh hu: the priest shall examine — if the nithak has spread in the skin, the priest need not look for yellow hair in it — he is unclean. When the spreading sign is present, it overrides the need for the yellow-hair sign. This diagnostic hierarchy shows that spreading is the more definitive indicator — the yellow hair was needed for initial diagnosis but spreading alone is sufficient for re-diagnosis.
37: ve-im-beinav amad hanithak vesear shahor tzamach bo nikra hanithak tahor hu vetiharah hakkohen: but if in his sight the nithak has stayed and black hair has grown in it — the nithak is healed. Sear shahor (black hair) indicates complete regrowth of normal hair, a sign that the condition has fully resolved. The regrowth of dark, healthy hair visibly replaces the fine yellow hair of active nithak — a positive diagnostic sign of healing. The priest declares clean.
38: ve-ish o isha ki yihyeh beohr besaram behaqot behaqot levanot: a man or woman who has in the skin of the body white-dull spots (behaqot levanot). Bohaq (v39) is the term for this presentation. Behaqot (plural of behaq) refers to dull, pale-white spots — from a root suggesting dimness or pallor rather than the bright white of baheret. This is a separate category from tzaraat.
39: veraah hakkohen vehineh beohr besaram behaqot kehot levanot bohaq hu parach baohr tahor hu: the priest examines — if the dull-white spots on the skin are dim-white, it is bohaq (tetter/freckle) — it has erupted in the skin; he is clean. Bohaq appears only here in the Bible; it is classified as a mild, harmless condition that does not require isolation or treatment. Its inclusion establishes that not every white skin condition is tzaraat — the diagnostic system discriminates between similar presentations based on specific observable criteria.
40: veish ki yimaret rosho qerach hu tahor hu: a man whose head has lost hair — he is qerach (bald at the back of the head); he is clean. Qerach refers to posterior/occipital baldness. The explicit clean declaration for natural baldness establishes a category distinction: natural hair loss is not a symptom of ritual impurity. The chapter carefully separates pathological from non-pathological presentations.
41: ve-im mipeat panav yimaret rosho gibe'ach hu tahor hu: if the hair has fallen from the forehead side — he is gibe'ach (bald at the forehead/front); he is clean. Gibe'ach refers to frontal baldness. Both posterior and frontal baldness are declared clean — natural alopecia of any type is explicitly not tzaraat. This prevents the diagnostic category from being applied too broadly.
42: ve-im yihyeh baqarach o vegibbe'ach nega levanah adamdemet: but if in the bald spot (qerach or gibbe'ach) there is a white-reddish mark. The presence of a specific lesion in the bald area, rather than natural baldness itself, triggers the diagnostic protocol. The white-reddish mark on a bald scalp is evaluated as potential tzaraat. The condition of the bald skin, not the baldness itself, determines ritual status.
43: veraah oto hakkohen vehineh sebeth-hanega lebana adamdemet beqarchato o begibbe'achto kemareeh tzaraat ohr habasar: the priest examines — if the white-reddish swelling of the mark in his bald-back or bald-forehead looks like the appearance of tzaraat of the body skin, he is a tzaraat person. Kemareeh tzaraat ohr habasar (like the appearance of tzaraat of the skin of the body) — the bald-scalp lesion is evaluated by comparison to the established tzaraat appearance. Analogy to known cases is the diagnostic method.
44: ish tzarua hu tameh hu: he is a man diseased with tzaraat (tzarua = tzaraat-afflicted) — he is unclean. The cohen tiameh oto berosh nega-o — the priest shall declare him thoroughly unclean; his mark is on his head. The emphatic construction (tiameh oto, pi'el intensive) combined with specifying the head location indicates that head-tzaraat carries a specific severity in the declaration protocol.
45: vehatzarua asher bo hanega begadav yihyu ferumim veroho yihyeh faru vehat-sifam ya'ateh vetumaah yiqra: and the tzaraat-afflicted person in whom the mark is — his garments shall be torn (ferumim), his head shall be uncovered (faru), he shall cover his upper lip (yachpeh), and he shall cry out: Unclean, Unclean (tameh tameh). Four visible markers of the tameh status: (1) torn garments — the sign of mourning (cf. Job 1:20, Joel 2:13); (2) uncovered/disheveled head (faru = to loosen, let hang freely); (3) covered upper lip — a sign of mourning or debasement (cf. Ezekiel 24:17, Micah 3:7); (4) the ritual cry tameh tameh — the doubled form for emphasis. The public self-announcement serves as warning to others and as the tzaruat person's own acknowledgment of their status. The mourning signs signal that tameh status is experienced as a kind of living death — exclusion from community, worship, and full life.
46: kol-yemei asher hanega vo yitma badad yeshev michutz lamachane moshavo: all the days that the mark is in him he shall be unclean — he shall dwell badad (alone, apart) and his dwelling shall be michutz lamachane (outside the camp). Badad (alone, in isolation) — the same root appears in Lamentations 1:1 where personified Jerusalem sits badad (alone) in exile. The tzaraat isolation is described with the same vocabulary of desolation and exile. Michutz lamachane (outside the camp) is the zone of maximum separation — where corpses, human waste, and those under judgment were placed (cf. Numbers 5:2-3; Exodus 29:14). The isolation removes the person from the sacred community surrounding the tabernacle. This geography of exclusion is deliberately structured around proximity to God's dwelling: the closer to YHWH, the higher the purity required.
47: vehabeged ki yihyeh vo nega tzaraat: and when a garment has in it a mark of tzaraat. Verses 47-59 extend the tzaraat regulations to fabric — wool, linen, or leather. The application of tzaraat categories to inanimate objects (garments, later houses in Leviticus 14) is one of the most distinctive features of the Levitical system. This extension is not biologically plausible — fabric does not contract infectious disease in the modern sense — which points toward the ritual/symbolic nature of tzaraat: it is a category of visible corruption/contamination that affects persons, garments, and structures in a spiritually analogous way.
48: bishti oba'erev letsemer o lefishtan o beor o bechol-melechet or: in the warp or weft of wool or of linen, or in leather or any article of leather. The precision of specifying both shti (warp threads) and erev (weft threads) demonstrates the detail of the inspection system. The tzaraat can appear in either structural element of woven fabric — the lengthwise or crosswise threads — and either location triggers the same examination protocol.
49: vehayah hanega yaraqraq o adamdam babeged o baohr o bashti o baerev o bechol-keli-or: and if the mark in the garment or leather or warp or weft or any article of leather is yaraqraq (greenish-yellow) or adamdam (reddish). Two color terms distinguish fabric tzaraat from skin tzaraat (which was white/bright or white-reddish). Yaraqraq (from yereq = green; the doubled form indicates intensity) = greenish. Adamdam (from adom = red; doubled for intensity) = reddish. These colors may indicate mold (green-black fungi) or oxidation on fabric. The color-based diagnostic for fabric parallels the appearance-based diagnostic for skin.
50: veraah hakkohen et-hanega vehibdil et-hanega shivat yamim: the priest shall examine the mark and isolate the marked item for seven days. The same 7-day observation period applied to persons with uncertain skin conditions is applied to potentially contaminated garments. The priest isolates the garment just as he isolates a person — the diagnostic protocol is structurally identical whether the subject is human skin or woven fiber.
51: veraah et-hanega bayom hashevii ki farash hanega babeged o bashti o baerev o baohr: if on the seventh day the mark has spread in the garment or warp or weft or leather — it is a tzaraat memaareret (malignant/fretting tzaraat) — it is unclean (tameh hu). Memaareret (from marar = to be bitter, to cause harm, to fret/corrode) describes an active, eating tzaraat — the disease that is spreading and consuming the material. The garment is declared tameh and must be burned (Leviticus 13:52).
52: vesaraf et-habeged: he shall burn the garment — wool or linen warp or weft, or any leather article with the malignant tzaraat — it shall be burned with fire. Burning is the most complete form of decontamination: it removes the object entirely from the realm of potential contamination. The destruction of contaminated garments rather than cleaning them reflects the irreversibility of tzaraat memaareret — once active spreading corruption has been established, the object cannot be remediated.
53: ve-im yireeh hakkohen vehineh lo farash hanega babeged: but if the priest examines and the mark has not spread in the garment. The non-spreading condition leads to a second option: washing and re-examination rather than immediate burning.
54: vetzivah hakkohen vechibesu et asher-bo hanega vehibdilo shivat yamim sheniyot: the priest shall command that the affected part be washed (chibesu) and isolated another seven days. Washing attempts remediation before destruction — the grace of trying to restore before condemning, parallel to the 7+7 day observation for human cases.
55: veraah hakkohen acharei hikabes et-hanega vehineh lo-hafach hanega et-einav vehanega lo farash tameh hu ba-esh tisrof: if after washing the mark has not changed its color/appearance and has not spread — it is unclean, burn it in fire, whether the affected part is the inner side or outer side. Lo hafach et-einav (has not changed its eye/appearance) — the mark's appearance is the ongoing diagnostic criterion. Even if it has not spread, the unchanged appearance after washing indicates persistent contamination requiring destruction. Pechemet hi (it is a fretting, whether in the back or face) — the location within the garment is noted but does not change the verdict.
56: ve-im raah hakkohen vehineh kehah hanega acharei hibakso: but if after washing the mark has dimmed/faded (kehah), the priest shall tear it out from the garment or from the leather. The fading indicates partial remediation — the contamination is diminishing but not fully cleared. The response is surgical removal of the affected portion rather than destroying the entire garment.
57: ve-im yereh od babeged o bashti o baerev o bechol keli-or tzaraat porach hi: but if it appears again in the garment or warp or weft or any leather article — it is tzaraat bursting forth (porach) — burn the item in fire. The recurrence of the mark after partial remediation demonstrates that the contamination is persistent and not merely superficial. At that point, destruction is the only remaining option. Porach (bursting out) describes a vigorous, recurring contamination that resists treatment.
58: vehabeged o hashti o-ha'erev o kol keli-haor asher tichabes vesfar mehem hanega uchubes sheniyot vetaher: but the garment, or warp, or weft, or any leather article that is washed and the mark is gone from it — it shall be washed a second time and shall be clean. The double washing (first remediation attempt + second confirming wash) required before declaring clean mirrors the double 7-day isolation for uncertain human cases. The thoroughness of the process ensures that clean declarations are not made prematurely.
59: zot torat nega-hatzaraat beged hashemer o-hafishtan o hashti o ha-erev o kol-keli-or letaharo o letameo: this is the law (torah) of the mark of tzaraat in a garment of wool or linen, in warp or weft or any leather article, to pronounce it clean or to pronounce it unclean. Torah (instruction, law) here closes the chapter as a legislative summary — the diagnostic procedures of Leviticus 13:47-58 constitute a torah, a formal body of instruction. The chapter as a whole (Leviticus 13:1-59) establishes the priestly diagnostic system for tzaraat in persons (Leviticus 13:1-46) and objects (Leviticus 13:47-59) — a comprehensive framework for managing the ritual contamination category across the material world. The priest's function throughout is not healing but discernment: to recognize, name, and properly classify what is present, then pronounce the appropriate verdict.
